title: Flip-Flops: When Index Membership Doesn’t Last  (CFA Institute Research Foundation Series) | Research Affiliates
gist: Rob Arnott argues that index 'flip-flops'—companies added then deleted from major indexes, or deleted then re-added—are common and expose a structural buy-high/sell-low flaw in cap-weighted indexing, with sharply asymmetric returns favoring deletions over additions.

insights.claim: Companies moving in and out of major indexes are common: many firms added to major indexes are later deleted, while a meaningful share of deletions eventually regain membership.
insights.speaker: Rob Arnott
insights.so_what: Index membership is far less stable than passive investors assume, so turnover-driven trades are a recurring, systematic feature rather than rare noise worth ignoring.
insights.quote: Many companies added to major indexes are later deleted, while a meaningful share of deletions eventually regain membership.

insights.claim: Cap-weighted indexing mechanically buys high and sells low because companies typically enter indexes after strong price gains and exit after prolonged declines.
insights.speaker: Rob Arnott
insights.so_what: The index-construction rule itself imposes a momentum-chasing tax on passive investors, an argument for valuation-aware or fundamentally weighted alternatives.
insights.quote: Companies often enter indexes after strong price gains and exit after prolonged declines, which can cause index investors to automatically buy high and sell low.

insights.claim: Valuation matters more than index membership, since inclusion is a backward-looking 'prestige' moment rather than a guarantee of future returns.
insights.speaker: Rob Arnott
insights.so_what: Investors should weight securities on valuation rather than treat index addition as a buy signal, reframing the passive-vs-active debate.
insights.quote: Index inclusion is a backward-looking “prestige” moment, not a guarantee of future returns, while deletions may present opportunities rather than automatic sell signals.

themes.theme: Asymmetric returns of additions vs deletions

insights.claim: Addition flip-flops typically underperform the market by two-thirds after inclusion, making newly added index members poor forward bets.
insights.speaker: Rob Arnott
insights.so_what: Buying stocks precisely because they just joined an index has historically been a losing trade, so investors can fade or underweight fresh additions.
insights.quote: Addition flip-flops typically underperform the market by two-thirds after inclusion
insights.locator: Flip-flop return patterns are highly asymmetric

insights.claim: Deletion flip-flops often generate nearly three times the benchmark returns before rejoining an index, so deletions may present opportunities rather than automatic sell signals.
insights.speaker: Rob Arnott
insights.so_what: Deleted names are a contrarian hunting ground; forced index sellers may be handing outperformance to buyers willing to hold through the exit.
insights.quote: deletion flip-flops often generate nearly three times the benchmark returns before rejoining an index
insights.locator: Flip-flop return patterns are highly asymmetric

insights.claim: The analysis is the final installment in a three-part series adapted from 'The Active Side of Indexing,' the authors' 2026 contribution to the CFA Institute Research Foundation.
insights.speaker: Rob Arnott
insights.so_what: Signals a peer-reviewed, foundational body of work rather than a one-off blog, lending weight to the anti-cap-weight thesis for allocators.
insights.locator: This article is the final installment
